CHAMBERS OF 
MUNICIPAL COURT 
S~~~TA :MONICA JUDICIAL DISTRICT 
..JOSEPH W. CHANDLER 
JUDGE 
1725 MAIN STREET 
SANTA MONICA, CALIFORNIA 90401 
February 7, 1977 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
State of California 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, Ca. 95814 
Attention: 
Mr. Kenneth Finney, Director 
Legal Division 
Dear Mr. Finney: 
Pursuant to our telephone conversation of this morning, I am re-
questing advice fran you prrsuant to Section 83114 Government Cede, sub-
division b , as added by 1976 statutes. 
I know there is a time problem, 
but I hope that you can get the answers back to me prior to .March 1. 
1. 
Does my prrchase of gocds and/or services fran a can-
mercial establishment effected by my use of a bank 
credit card, e.g. Bankamericard or Mastercharge, involve 
a "loan" within the meaning of Section 82030 Government 
Cede and thus reportable by me? 
I would concede that apparently the receipt of cash by me directly 
fran my bank upon my presentment to the bank of my bank credit card for a 
cash advance would be a "loan" within the meaning of the above section. 
Likewise, apparently, a cash advance by my bank to my checking account, 
pursuant to a pre-existing agreement between the bank and me whereby the 
bank agrees to cover any checks written to carmercial establisl1rr:ents (up 
to a certain credit limit) whenever the balance in my account is insuffi-
cient to cover a particular check, is a "loan" within the meaning of 
Section 82030. 
An example of the latter is Security Pacific National 
funk's "Ready Reserve" account and Bank of California's "M::mey Check" 
account. 
2. 
Does my purchase of gocds am/or services fran a commercial 
establishment made via the establishment's charge account 
involve a 
"loan" within the meaning of Section 82030? 
Incidentally, you have informally rend.ered the opinion over the phone 
that the answer would be "no" to the question raised in each of the above 
two mnubered paragraphs. 
- 1 -


3. 
Are fees for officiating at weddings re:rx>rtable as either 
gifts or incane when the weddings are performed on an 
occasional ba.sis, the fee charged in each instance (if 
not done without fee) is not dispro:rx>rtionate in my view 
to the time an:l expense in going to the weddin::J site, is 
in each instance more than $25.00 but considerably less 
than $250.00, although the total of said fees over the 
re:rx>rting pericrl is in excess of $250. OO? 
(I und.erstan:l 
fran you informally that your view is that they are not 
so re:rx>rtable und.er the conditions herein mentioned i that 
each weddin::J is discrete.) 
4. 
Are monthly payments to me fran the Los Angeles County 
Flnployees' RetirEment Board (fran previous County Employ-
ment) reportable as incane within the meaning of Government 
Ccrle Section 82030? 
(You have informally indicated it is 
not so reportable on the ground. that it ba.sically is 
"salary. . . received fran a state or local government agency" 
within the meaning of Section 82030 (b) (2) .) 
(Likewise, that 
the source of said payments is not an "investment" within 
the meaning of Section 82034 Government Ccrle because the 
County RetirEment Board is not a "business entity ". ) 
(Like-
wise, I und.erstar:d that my investment in the Judges' State 
Retirement Fund likewise is not an II investment" wi thin the 
meaning of Section 82034.) 
5 . 
Are Federal and State personal incane tax refund.s re:rx>rtable 
as "incane" within the meaning of the words "payment" or 
"reba.te"? 
(You have advised me informally that such refun:is 
are not considered as "incane II within the meaning of 
Section 82030.) 
6. 
Is "interest" paid to me by the Federal government for delay 
in effecting said refun:is, "incane" within the meaning of 
Section 82030? 
As mentioned above, I know there is a time problEm, but I would appre-
ciate your pranpt attention to this matter. 
~ours very truly, and hurriedly, 
Jw::::bj

sections.question:
1. Does my purchase of goods and/or services from a commercial establishment effected by my use of a bank credit card, e.g. Bankamericard or Mastercharge, involve a "loan" within the meaning of Section 82030 Government Code and thus reportable by me?

2. Does my purchase of goods and/or services from a commercial establishment made via the establishment's charge account involve a "loan" within the meaning of Section 82030?

3. Are fees for officiating at weddings reportable as either gifts or income when the weddings are performed on an occasional basis, the fee charged in each instance (if not done without fee) is not disproportionate in my view to the time and expense in going to the wedding site, is in each instance more than $25.00 but considerably less than $250.00, although the total of said fees over the reporting period is in excess of $250.00?

4. Are monthly payments to me from the Los Angeles County Employees' Retirement Board (from previous County Employment) reportable as income within the meaning of Government Code Section 82030?

5. Are Federal and State personal income tax refunds reportable as "income" within the meaning of the words "payment" or "rebate"?

6. Is "interest" paid to me by the Federal government for delay in effecting said refunds, "income" within the meaning of Section 82030?
sections.conclusion: Based on telephone conversation of February 23, 1977: (1) Credit card purchases do not create a political form/loan if paid off within billing cycle with no interest; (2) gifts of $50-$75 are not reportable income and individual wedding ceremonies need not be aggregated across the $250 reporting threshold; (3) Los Angeles County Employees' Retirement Board payments are "salary...from a state or local government agency" and are not reportable as income; (4) Federal/State tax refunds would be reportable if $250 or more.
sections.question_synthetic: Judge Chandler requests FPPC advice on whether various financial transactions and payments—including credit card purchases, retirement payments, wedding fees, and tax refunds—constitute reportable income or loans under the Fair Political Practices Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that credit card purchases without interest charges do not constitute reportable loans, wedding officiating fees need not be aggregated for reporting purposes, retirement payments are excluded from income reporting, and tax refunds are reportable only if they meet the $250 threshold.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is heavily damaged/degraded with significant OCR errors and text loss. Multiple sections are illegible or partially missing. Both the incoming request letter and FPPC response are present in the document. The response letter references a phone conversation and informal opinions previously given. Confidence is moderate due to document quality, but the main substance of questions and conclusions can be discerned.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does my purchase of goods and/or services from a commercial establishment effected by my use of a bank credit card, e.g. Bankamericard or Mastercharge, involve a "loan" within the meaning of Section 82030 Government Code and thus reportable by me?

2. Does my purchase of goods and/or services from a commercial establishment made via the establishment's charge account involve a "loan" within the meaning of Section 82030?

3. Are fees for officiating at weddings reportable as either gifts or income when the weddings are performed on an occasional basis, the fee charged in each instance (if not done without fee) is not disproportionate in my view to the time and expense in going to the wedding site, is in each instance more than $25.00 but considerably less than $250.00, although the total of said fees over the reporting period is in excess of $250.00?

4. Are monthly payments to me from the Los Angeles County Employees' Retirement Board (from previous County Employment) reportable as income within the meaning of Government Code Section 82030?

5. Are Federal and State personal income tax refunds reportable as "income" within the meaning of the words "payment" or "rebate"?

6. Is "interest" paid to me by the Federal government for delay in effecting said refunds, "income" within the meaning of Section 82030?

CONCLUSION: Based on telephone conversation of February 23, 1977: (1) Credit card purchases do not create a political form/loan if paid off within billing cycle with no interest; (2) gifts of $50-$75 are not reportable income and individual wedding ceremonies need not be aggregated across the $250 reporting threshold; (3) Los Angeles County Employees' Retirement Board payments are "salary...from a state or local government agency" and are not reportable as income; (4) Federal/State tax refunds would be reportable if $250 or more.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC advice letter responding to Judge W. Chandler's six questions regarding what financial transactions and payments must be reported under California's Fair Political Practices Act, addressing credit cards, retirement benefits, wedding fees, and tax refunds. The FPPC provides clarification that most of these items are either not reportable or require aggregation to meet reporting thresholds.
